E1.1 complete. Fixed pack.dart's bare-pack invocation: the old --target host --linked flags were invalid (no --target flag exists in bare-pack); replaced with --preset android (bare-pack's shorthand for --linked + all 4 android hosts), matching holepunchto/bare-android's own packApp Gradle task. Extracted buildBundle(pkgRoot) -> int (bare-pack's real exit code) out of main() for testability. Wired assets/pear-end.bundle into flutter_pear's pubspec.yaml as a Flutter asset (documented as a committed/versioned artifact in CLAUDE.md, matching the THIRD_PARTY_LICENSES convention).

Also fixed 3 pre-existing bugs discovered while getting the validation gate to run (Flutter/Dart SDK had never been installed in this environment before, so `melos run analyze`/`flutter test` had never actually been exercised):
- flutter_pear_example/pubspec.yaml: unquoted colon in `description` broke YAML parsing for the whole workspace (melos bootstrap/pub get failed entirely).
- flutter_pear_bare/lib/src/bare_worklet.dart: `_onIpc`'s type (`Future<ByteData>? Function(ByteData?)`) didn't satisfy `BasicMessageChannel<ByteData>.setMessageHandler`'s required `Future<ByteData> Function(ByteData?)` shape -- flutter analyze errored. Fixed by making the channel `BasicMessageChannel<ByteData?>` and the handler `Future<ByteData?> _onIpc(...) async`.
- pack.dart's pkgRoot resolution via `File.fromUri(Platform.script).parent.parent` broke under `dart run` (resolves to a .dart_tool/pub/bin/ kernel cache path, not bin/pack.dart's real location) -- switched to `Directory.current.path` per the documented "run from packages/flutter_pear" convention.

Ran a 5-angle parallel code review after the initial fix; findings converged on: exit-code fidelity lost (fixed: buildBundle now returns bare-pack's real exit code instead of a collapsed bool), and the new buildBundle test hard-failing on machines without bare-pack installed (fixed: skip guard via ProcessException catch + markTestSkipped). Also fixed a CLAUDE.md docs gap (dart run flutter_pear:pack needs cwd = packages/flutter_pear, wasn't stated).

Test evidence: `melos run analyze` -> 0 issues across all 3 packages. `flutter test` in flutter_pear -> 6/6 passed (incl. new buildBundle test). `flutter test` in flutter_pear_bare -> 2/2 passed. `dart run flutter_pear:pack` succeeds from a clean run, produces packages/flutter_pear/assets/pear-end.bundle (2.4KB, valid bare-bundle JSON format).

IMPORTANT FOR COMMIT: packages/flutter_pear/assets/pear-end.bundle is currently untracked in git (new file, generated by pack). Per project convention (documented in CLAUDE.md) it's a committed/versioned artifact, not a build-time-generated one -- must be included in the commit alongside the code changes, or a fresh checkout will fail to build (pubspec.yaml now declares it as a required Flutter asset).

Toolchain note: this environment had no Flutter/Dart/melos/bare-pack installed. Installed Flutter 3.44.4 stable (git clone --depth 1 -b stable into ~/development/flutter, added to ~/.zshrc PATH), melos 8.0.0 via dart pub global activate, bare-pack 2.1.3 + bare-link 3.3.0 via npm -g. Accepted Android SDK licenses.

E1.2 complete (build-only validation, see below). Added a fetchBareKit Gradle task to flutter_pear_bare/android/build.gradle that fetches the pinned Bare Kit v2.3.0 prebuild release from GitHub, extracts classes.jar + arm64-v8a-only .so (spike-grade, per scope), and wires them as a compile dependency + jniLibs source dir -- mirrors holepunchto/bare-android's own libs/bare-kit/ Gradle pattern rather than treating it as a proper AAR (matches upstream).

Hydrated flutter_pear_example's Android runner (flutter create --platforms=android .) and removed the generated boilerplate test/widget_test.dart (referenced a nonexistent MyApp symbol from the default counter template -- would have broken melos run test for reasons unrelated to this app).

Moved the INTERNET permission to flutter_pear_bare's own (committed) library AndroidManifest.xml instead of the example app's manifest as originally scoped -- discovered mid-task that packages/flutter_pear_example/android/ is NOT committed to git per this repo's own documented convention ("Example platform runners aren't committed -- flutter create hydrates them", CLAUDE.md + README + example README all say this), so any manifest edit made directly on the example app would be silently lost for every other developer who re-hydrates. Putting it on the library manifest instead means it auto-merges into any consuming app with zero manual steps -- simpler than the originally-planned "spike now, E4.3 fixes it properly later" path, and actually front-runs part of E4.3's own stated goal.

Code review (2 parallel agents) on the Gradle wiring found and I fixed 4 real issues before closing:
- jniLibs merge tasks (mergeDebugJniLibFolders et al.) had no explicit dependency on fetchBareKit, only incidental preBuild ordering -- same bug class that made mozilla/rust-android-gradle silently ship AARs missing their .so when built outside a full app assemble (rust-android-gradle#85). Fixed: explicit tasks.matching(mergeJniLibFolders).configureEach { dependsOn(fetchBareKit) }. Verified by building :flutter_pear_bare:assembleDebug standalone (deleted the bare-kit cache first) and confirming the output AAR actually contains jni/arm64-v8a/libbare-kit.so.
- fetchBareKit only declared outputs.file(classesJar), not the jni directory it also produces -- fixed with outputs.dir().
- Interrupted download (Ctrl+C, CI timeout) would leave a truncated prebuilds.zip that `exists()` treats as complete forever, poisoning every subsequent build until manual deletion -- fixed with .part-file + atomic rename.
- bareKitVersion wasn't part of the cache path, so bumping the pinned version wouldn't invalidate a stale cached zip from the old version -- fixed by scoping the cache dir to build/bare-kit/${bareKitVersion}/.
Left as documented, deferred debt (commented in the file): no checksum/signature verification of the downloaded binary, and the task isn't Configuration-Cache compatible (calls project.ant/project.copy from doLast) -- both explicitly epic E4 scope, not this spike's.

Bundling the unused .so doesn't matter yet (Android won't load it just from being in the APK), but E1.3's actual worklet boot (System.loadLibrary + JNI calls) is the real test of whether this works on our only hardware, once storage is freed.

E1.3 complete. Rewrote FlutterPearBarePlugin.kt to boot a real to.holepunch.bare.kit.Worklet from the bundled pear-end (loaded as bytes via AssetManager + FlutterAssets.getAssetFilePathBySubpath, not Worklet's InputStream overload -- that one calls source.reset() with no preceding mark(), which only works by accident for certain stream types) and pipe its IPC bidirectionally to the existing flutter_pear_bare/ipc BasicMessageChannel. Native echo removed entirely. Dart API (bare_worklet.dart) untouched, per the issue's own constraint.

Hot-restart reattach: the Worklet/IPC pair live in a companion object (JVM-static), not the plugin instance, since a Dart hot restart tears down/recreates the engine (and often the plugin object) without killing the Android process. start() checks for an existing companion worklet and just re-arms the read loop instead of booting a second worklet.

suspend()/resume()/terminate() call bare-kit's real native APIs (no TODO/no-op needed -- bare-kit exposes genuine suspend(linger)/resume()/terminate() methods, better than the "no-op with TODO otherwise" the issue anticipated). terminate() explicitly closes the IPC before terminating the Worklet (bare-kit doesn't order this itself; an IPC built on an already-terminated worklet touches freed native memory).

Researched bare-kit's actual Java API by reading Worklet.java/IPC.java source directly from holepunchto/bare-kit and holepunchto/bare-android's real MainActivity.kt (not guessing) -- confirmed byte I/O goes through a separate `IPC` class (readable/writable poll callbacks backed by a native ALooper captured at IPC-construction time), not Worklet.push (which is a separate request/response RPC channel, not bulk byte streaming).

Code review (2 parallel agents: correctness + CLAUDE.md conventions) found and I fixed 5 real issues:
- relayFromWorklet()'s read loop recursed directly on the call stack when IPC.read() delivers synchronously (data already buffered) -- a worklet streaming a continuous burst could blow the stack. Fixed: the continuation now posts through Handler(Looper.getMainLooper()) instead of calling itself directly, so each chunk gets its own Looper iteration.
- Hot-restart reattach had no guard against a stale closure (armed by the OLD plugin instance before detach) forwarding worklet data to the now-dead BasicMessageChannel after detach -- silent data loss. Fixed: an instance-level `attached` flag set false in onDetachedFromEngine, checked before ipc.send().
- terminateWorklet() nulled the companion refs but didn't stop an already-armed read closure from touching the closed/freed IPC on its next fire -- unverified use-after-free risk. Fixed: the read closure captures its own `activeIpc` and checks `workletIpc !== activeIpc` (identity, not null-check) before doing anything, so a stale closure from a superseded generation becomes an inert no-op instead of re-arming on a dead handle.
- `catch (e: Exception)` around Worklet(null)/w.start() missed JNI-level failures like UnsatisfiedLinkError (Error subtype, e.g. from an ABI mismatch) -- widened to `catch (e: Throwable)`. (A genuine native segfault still can't be caught from Kotlin at all -- documented in the comment, not fixable here.)
- suspend()/resume() silently no-op'd and still reported result.success(null) when no worklet was running, masking a real state-desync bug per this project's "errors must travel" convention -- now reports result.error("worklet_not_started", ...) instead. (Confirmed unreachable via the supported Dart API today, since BareWorklet already gates on _state first -- this is a defensive backstop, not a live bug fix.)

Second reviewer decompiled the actual fetched bare-kit v2.3.0 classes.jar with javap to verify (not just assert) that IPC.readable()'s poll-callback slot is a single overwritten field, not a fan-out list -- confirming the reattach logic can't duplicate or misdirect frames. Also confirmed worklet.onCrash (the "errors must travel" JS-crash-to-Dart-exception path) is correctly out of scope here -- already filed separately as flutter_pear-7be.6 (E2.6) under the RPC contract epic, locked as native-only crash detection; nothing in this diff creates a false impression that JS crashes are handled (the try/catch here only wraps the synchronous Kotlin boot path).

BLOCKED (same as E1.2): on-device byte-flow verification. Deferred to flutter_pear-dpl -- the only physical device (BYD head unit) has no free storage. Also carrying forward from E1.2: bare-kit's own prebuild declares minSdkVersion 31; our device is API 29. This task is the first one where that mismatch could actually matter at runtime (System.loadLibrary + JNI init now genuinely execute on start, unlike E1.2 where the .so just sat unused in the APK) -- will surface as a concrete pass/fail once storage is freed and flutter_pear-dpl can proceed.

Test evidence: `melos run analyze` -> 0 issues across all 3 packages. `flutter test` in flutter_pear_bare -> 2/2 passed (bare_worklet_test.dart untouched, still green -- it only exercises the Dart-side mocked channel, doesn't reach this Kotlin code, so it can't regress from these changes but also doesn't cover them). `flutter build apk --debug --target-platform android-arm64` succeeds from packages/flutter_pear_example, confirming the Kotlin compiles and links cleanly against the real bare-kit Worklet/IPC classes.

E1.4 complete. pear-end/index.js now joins a hardcoded Hyperswarm topic (crypto.hash of a fixed string, via hypercore-crypto -- already a declared dep) automatically on worklet boot, relays bytes bidirectionally between swarm connections and IPC (swarm.connection/connection.data/connection.close events with topic+peer fields matching the ALREADY-DESIGNED Dart PearSwarm/PearConnection contract in swarm.dart, even though wiring that Dart API up is a later epic -- costs nothing to match now), wires connection.write to route by peer, and adds a debug.forceError RPC method (with a .code property) so the JS error path is testable end-to-end through the existing err envelope (extended to include err.code, which the envelope was missing before).

Discovered and fixed a real gate-blocking gap while validating: pear-end's bundle now resolves hyperswarm's native addons (sodium-native, udx-native, bare-fs, bare-os, bare-url, bare-type, bare-inspect) as `linked:` specifiers (confirmed via grep on the rebuilt bundle), but E1.2's Gradle wiring only ever fetched the bare-kit runtime itself -- nothing linked these addons' actual .so files. Without this, the worklet would fail at require('hyperswarm') time on-device regardless of storage. Added a linkNativeAddons Gradle task (flutter_pear_bare/android/build.gradle) that runs bare-link --preset android against pear-end's already-npm-installed node_modules, filtered to arm64-v8a (spike scope, matching E1.2's own convention), wired into jniLibs.srcDirs alongside bare-kit's own. Verified: standalone `flutter build apk --debug` now bundles all 7 addons + libbare-kit.so (11 total .so files in lib/arm64-v8a/).

Licensing check (CLAUDE.md requirement): all 59 modules now bundled are MIT/Apache-2.0/ISC -- no GPL/AGPL/LGPL/MPL/SSPL found (verified via word-boundary grep on the regenerated THIRD_PARTY_LICENSES, not just a license-field skim).

Fixed a real regression in my own E1.1-era pack_test.dart: it copied only pear-end/index.js into an isolated temp dir for the buildBundle test, which worked when the stub had no requires but broke the moment index.js started requiring real npm packages (MODULE_NOT_FOUND). Fixed by symlinking node_modules from the real pear-end/ into the temp dir, plus a graceful skip (matching the existing bare-pack-missing skip pattern) if node_modules isn't installed.

Code review (1 agent, 6 angles) found and I fixed 3 real issues, confirmed 3 others as non-issues after tracing the actual RPC/IPC contract end to end:
- Stale Hyperswarm connection Map entry: the 'close' handler deleted/reported close by peer-key only, with no identity check against the actual conn object -- if a peer reconnects fast enough that the new connection's 'connection' event lands before the old one's 'close' fires, the stale close would both evict the live Map entry (causing a subsequent connection.write to wrongly throw "unknown peer") AND send a spurious connection.close event to Dart that would tear down the still-live PearConnection. Fixed with an identity check (`connections.get(peer) !== conn`) gating both the delete and the event send.
- Gradle's onlyIf skip (missing pear-end/node_modules) claimed in a comment to "match the license collector's skip-with-stderr-note behavior" but emitted no actual note -- a CI/dev machine without npm install would silently ship an APK missing all native addons with zero build-time signal. Fixed: onlyIf now logger.warn()s explaining exactly what's missing and what breaks.
- linkNativeAddons declared outputs but no inputs, so Gradle's incremental build could mark it up-to-date and skip re-running bare-link even after a pear-end dependency bump (unlike the sibling fetchBareKit task, which is already version-scoped for this exact reason) -- fixed by declaring package.json/package-lock.json as inputs.
- Confirmed non-issues: early send()/IPC.write() calls at module-load time are not lost (Bare Kit's IPC pipe buffers independent of when the Dart-side read loop arms); err.code survives as a plain property and JSON.stringify correctly omits it as an absent key (not a literal "undefined") when unset; the rootDir-relative path to pear-end/ is fragile for hypothetical non-monorepo consumers but resolves correctly for this repo's actual layout today (documented as known E4-scope debt, not fixed now).
- Introduced and immediately caught (via the actual rebuild) a Groovy syntax bug in my own fix: backtick-escaping in a double-quoted Groovy string isn't valid syntax and broke the whole build.gradle -- caught because I validate every change with a real build, not just a diff read.

Not fixed (out of E1.4's file scope, noted for whoever wires up PearSwarm for real): swarm.dart's `_byKey.remove(p['peer'])?._close()` has the same by-key-only removal pattern the JS fix addresses -- but since the JS side no longer SENDS a stale connection.close event, the Dart-side trigger condition is eliminated at the source; Dart itself is unmodified per this issue's stated file scope.

BLOCKED (same as E1.2/E1.3): full on-device round-trip verification (the actual two-peer swarm join/relay/forced-error over real hardware) remains deferred to flutter_pear-dpl -- BYD device storage. What IS verified without a device: bundle produces correctly, bare-link produces real per-ABI .so files, full apk build succeeds with all addons present, and the JS module's logic was reviewed end-to-end against the exact Dart-side contract it feeds.

Test evidence: `melos run analyze` -> 0 issues across all 3 packages. `flutter test` in flutter_pear -> 6/6 passed (incl. fixed buildBundle test). `flutter test` in flutter_pear_bare -> 2/2 passed. `flutter build apk --debug --target-platform android-arm64` succeeds, APK contains all 7 linked native addons + libbare-kit.so. `dart run flutter_pear:pack` succeeds, produces a 700KB bundle (up from 2.4KB) with 59 THIRD_PARTY_LICENSES entries, all permissive-licensed.

Storage blocker resolved: developer freed BYD device storage (~2GB -> 40GB free, confirmed 2026-07-03). Re-ran on-device install: app installs and launches successfully on BYD (storage issue is gone), BUT hit a new, distinct, real bug -- Worklet.start() fails with a dlopen error for libnativehelper.so, specific to BYD's ROM (does not reproduce on an Android emulator). Tracked separately as flutter_pear-1y1 since it's a different root cause than what this issue was scoped to. Per developer decision (2026-07-03): use an Android emulator (Medium_Phone_API_35, already installed locally) as the primary dev/test target going forward; flutter_pear_example has been uninstalled from the BYD unit; only return to BYD hardware once things are stable on emulator.

Developer decision (2026-07-03): BYD AUTO head unit is removed from flutter_pear's supported/target device pool entirely -- not being pursued as a bug to fix. Root cause (BYD's automotive ROM linker namespace blocking libbare-kit.so's libnativehelper.so dependency) stays recorded above for reference, but no further investigation into BYD's ROM/linker config is planned. Local development and validation now targets the Android emulator (Medium_Phone_API_35 et al., already installed) as primary; the flutter_pear_example app was uninstalled from the BYD unit. Any future physical-device testing (E1.5 two-device gate, on-device checks) should use a different physical Android device, not BYD.

Authored packages/flutter_pear/lib/src/schema.dart (Dart constants: PearMethod, PearEventName, PearErrorCode, PearFrameType [reserved], PearHandshakeField [reserved]) and pear-end/schema.js (hand-kept JS mirror, no codegen per LOCKED decision). Replaced every method/event string literal in swarm.dart and pear-end/index.js with the shared constants; index.js's previously-uncoded 'unknown peer'/'unknown method' throws now carry proper err.code values (UNKNOWN_PEER/UNKNOWN_METHOD).

TESTS: new test/schema_test.dart -- asserts all 15 Dart constant values, and cross-checks pear-end/schema.js's literal values against them via a regex scan (catches the two hand-authored files drifting apart, since there's no codegen to guarantee sync). Fixed a regression this surfaced in test/pack_test.dart's buildBundle test (its isolated temp-dir fixture only copied index.js, not the new schema.js it requires -- bare-pack failed module resolution until fixed).

VALIDATION: melos run analyze clean (3/3 packages); flutter test green (8/8, flutter_pear); grep confirms zero remaining inline method/event literals in lib/ outside schema.dart itself; dart run flutter_pear:pack regenerates the bundle successfully (59 modules, all MIT/Apache-2.0/ISC per THIRD_PARTY_LICENSES); rebuilt flutter_pear_example debug APK and confirmed clean worklet boot on the Android emulator (no crash, 'Fully drawn').

REVIEW: independent agent review found no correctness bugs; two cosmetic nits (schema.js missing 'use strict', JS objects not frozen) -- applied the 'use strict' fix, skipped Object.freeze as unwarranted hardening.

Bonus finding while validating a sibling E4 ticket's release build against this same worklet: discovered and fixed a real R8 release-mode native crash (JNI 'mid == null' abort from libbare-kit.so's MessageQueue.nativePollOnce integration) via a consumer-rules.pro keep rule on flutter_pear_bare -- noted on flutter_pear-47k.3/47k.5 since it's their scope, not E2.1's, but landed here since it was blocking release-build validation entirely.

Library manifest already carried INTERNET (added during E1.4) with a comment explaining the auto-merge; example app manifest already carried no duplicate. This task's job was validating that end-to-end on a RELEASE build, which surfaced a real, separate bug: libbare-kit.so aborts on boot in release mode ('JNI DETECTED ERROR IN APPLICATION: mid == null', from its MessageQueue.nativePollOnce integration) because R8 strips to.holepunch.bare.kit's unobfuscated JNI-called methods with no keep rule protecting them. Fixed via packages/flutter_pear_bare/android/consumer-rules.pro (-keep class to.holepunch.bare.kit.** { *; }) wired through consumerProguardFiles, so every consuming app gets it automatically -- no manual app-dev step.

VALIDATION (emulator, per developer decision to use emulator over BYD hardware): flutter build apk --release --target-platform android-arm64 succeeds; aapt dump permissions on the release APK lists android.permission.INTERNET; adb shell dumpsys package confirms INTERNET granted=true; installed + launched on Medium_Phone_API_35 -- before the proguard fix this crashed immediately (SIGABRT, full tombstone captured); after the fix, clean boot, no crash, 'Fully drawn', process stays alive. melos run analyze clean (3/3 packages).

Added a per-call timeout to PearRpc.call() (schema.dart: PearRpcDefaults.callTimeout, default 10s, overridable per call) and centralized ALL completer resolution through one _settle(id, {result, error}) method -- the per-call Timer, _onFrame's real response handling, and dispose()'s fail-all loop each go through it, so exactly one of them ever resolves a given call and a late arrival after another already settled it is a silent no-op, not a double-complete crash. Introduced a minimal WorkletIpc interface (flutter_pear_bare) with just incoming/send so PearRpc depends on that instead of the concrete BareWorklet -- lets flutter_pear_test's planned in-memory fake (E3, already blocked on this) substitute for a real worklet in tests.

REVIEW (independent agent) caught two real gaps in the 'fail-pending-on-death' half of this ticket that I fixed before closing:
1. _worklet.send()'s Future was never awaited or error-handled -- if the worklet was already not-running at call() time, BareWorklet.send()'s StateError became an unhandled Zone error AND the call just sat for the full timeout before wrongly failing with RPC_TIMEOUT instead of a code reflecting what actually happened. Fixed: send()'s Future now has .catchError attached that immediately settles the call with a new PearErrorCode.sendFailed.
2. No dispose-guard on call() -- calling after dispose() registered a new completer+timer that would hang for the full timeout before failing. Fixed: a _disposed flag makes post-dispose calls fail immediately with PearErrorCode.workletDisposed via Future.error(), never touching _worklet.send at all.

TESTS (test/rpc_test.dart, new file): 11 cases -- normal ok/err responses, unanswered call times out with RPC_TIMEOUT, dispose fails all pending with WORKLET_DISPOSED, late response after timeout/after dispose both silently ignored (not a crash), send failure settles immediately with SEND_FAILED, call() after dispose() fails immediately without ever reaching send(). All fast (no test takes >20ms of simulated delay).

Also caught and fixed two bugs in my OWN test-writing during this: a dispose test that awaited dispose() BEFORE attaching expectLater() (Dart reports an error-completed Future with no listener yet as unhandled -- fixed by attaching the expectation first); and a 'late response after dispose' test that forgot to actually call dispose() in its own body (relied on tearDown, which runs too late) -- fixed by disposing explicitly inline.

VALIDATION: melos run analyze clean (3/3); flutter test green (19/19, flutter_pear); flutter_pear_bare's own test suite still green (WorkletIpc/@override additions are purely additive, confirmed no BareWorklet behavior change); dart run flutter_pear:pack regenerates the bundle; rebuilt + installed + launched flutter_pear_example on the Android emulator after every change in this ticket -- clean boot each time, no crash, 'Fully drawn'.

Added an explicit err.code -> exception-subtype registry: PearErrorCategory enum (connection/storage) in schema.dart, PearErrorCode.categories (a const Map<String, PearErrorCategory> with exactly 2 entries today: unknownPeer->connection, storageUnavailable->storage -- LOCKED as explicit entries, no prefix/substring heuristics), and a new pearExceptionFor(message, {code, stack}) factory in exceptions.dart that looks up the code and constructs PearConnectionException/PearStorageException/base PearException accordingly via an exhaustive Dart 3 switch expression. All 3 PearException construction sites in rpc.dart (timeout, worklet-err response, dispose) now route through this factory for consistency.

Added PearErrorCode.storageUnavailable ('STORAGE_UNAVAILABLE') as a RESERVED code -- nothing throws it yet since no storage features exist (E5 not started), but it gives the registry a second real category to test ahead of E5 actually using it, same pattern as E2.1's reserved frame-type/handshake constants.

TESTS: 3 new cases in test/rpc_test.dart -- a connection-class code (PearErrorCode.unknownPeer) throws PearConnectionException with message/code/stack all preserved; a storage-class code (storageUnavailable) throws PearStorageException likewise; an arbitrary unregistered code string throws the base PearException specifically verified to NOT be either subtype (isNot(isA<...>()) x2), with its code preserved. 19/19 tests green total.

REVIEW (independent agent): no correctness bugs. Confirmed the registry lookup is a genuine compile-time const map with correct value-based String equality (verified via a real jsonEncode/jsonDecode round-trip in the test, not object identity), the switch expression is truly exhaustive, and -- importantly -- traced that PearErrorCode.unknownPeer is not just theoretically mapped but ACTUALLY thrown by pear-end/index.js's connection.write handler today using the exact same wire string, so PearConnectionException fires on a real, reachable worklet error path right now, not just in the simulated test. Two optional nits applied: added the missing  assertion to the storage-class test for parity with the connection-class one, and ran dart format on all touched files (fixed line-wrapping in exceptions.dart/rpc.dart/rpc_test.dart).

VALIDATION: melos run analyze clean (3/3 packages); flutter test green (19/19, flutter_pear); dart run flutter_pear:pack regenerates the bundle; rebuilt + installed + launched flutter_pear_example on the Android emulator -- clean boot, no crash, 'Fully drawn'.

Every IPC frame is now prefixed with a 1-byte PearFrameType discriminator (0x00=JSON control, 0x01=raw/reserved) on both sides. rpc.dart: _encode prepends the byte; _onFrame reads it first, and anything unexpected -- an unrecognized discriminator byte, malformed JSON after a valid 0x00, a well-formed JSON value that isn't an object, or a well-formed JSON object matching neither a response (numeric id) nor an event (string ev) -- now emits a new PearEventName.rpcDiagnostic event via the existing events stream instead of vanishing silently (the exact bug this ticket exists to fix: rpc.dart previously did 'catch (_) { return; }' with zero observability). pear-end/index.js mirrors all of this: send() prepends FrameType.JSON, IPC.on('data') branches on the discriminator and calls a new diagnostic() helper for the same unrecognized-shape cases.

Found and fixed two real bugs while implementing this:
1. Independent review caught that I'd applied the 'never silent-drop' loudification inconsistently even within the same function -- I loudified 'valid JSON, not an object' but left an equally-malformed adjacent case ('valid JSON object, but neither response nor event shape') silently falling through, on both the Dart AND JS sides. Extended the same diagnostic treatment to both for consistency (new test: 'a well-formed JSON object matching neither response nor event is surfaced').
2. Found while touching the exact line: pear-end's pre-existing 'if (typeof frame.id !== "number") return' would throw an uncaught TypeError if a request frame's JSON body were literally 'null' (JSON.parse('null') -> JS null -> null.id throws) -- a pre-existing crash risk (bare-kit aborts the whole host process on uncaught JS exceptions) unrelated to but immediately adjacent to this ticket's own change. Real bug found and fixed mid-implementation: Gradle's incremental-build machinery marked fetchBareKit UP-TO-DATE once its outputs already existed, silently SKIPPING the checksum on every subsequent build -- confirmed by tampering one byte of the cached zip and watching the build succeed anyway. Fixed with outputs.upToDateWhen { false }, confirmed the same tamper now fails loud with the exact expected-vs-actual hash in a clear GradleException.

ABI matrix: bareKitAbis = [arm64-v8a, x86_64] (was arm64-v8a only). x86_64 covers desktop-class emulators (Apple Silicon's native-arm64 images already worked via arm64-v8a; this adds Intel/CI x86_64 support). armeabi-v7a/x86 (32-bit) explicitly excluded with a comment -- bare-kit's own minSdk 31 leaves no realistic 32-bit target regardless of distribution channel. Wired into both fetchBareKit's zip-extraction filter and linkNativeAddons's ABI-pruning filter (previously hardcoded to arm64-v8a in both places).

3. Version pinning: bareKitVersion was already single-sourced from E1.2; confirmed unchanged.

Implemented the session nonce + bundle-version attach handshake: pear-end generates a SESSION_NONCE (crypto.randomBytes) once at worklet boot and stamps it (envelope-level 'n' field, PearHandshakeField.envelopeNonce) on EVERY frame it sends. A new PearMethod.attachInfo request/response (deliberately NOT an event, so it answers correctly whether Dart is asking for the first time or re-asking after a hot-restart reattach to an already-running worklet) returns {nonce, bundleVersion}. Pear.start() always calls attach.info immediately after constructing PearRpc; on a bundleVersion mismatch against the generated kPearEndBundleVersion Dart constant, it disposes+terminates the worklet, boots a fresh one, and re-checks once -- if it STILL mismatches, throws PearException(BUNDLE_VERSION_MISMATCH) rather than silently proceeding. bundleVersion is a SHA-256 hash (truncated) of index.js + schema.js + package-lock.json, computed and written to both a gitignored pear-end/version.js (bundled in) and a committed lib/src/bundle_version.dart by a new pack.dart step -- content-based, not human-maintained, so ANY relevant change is auto-detected.

MAJOR DISCOVERY while validating this on-device: found and fixed a severe, PRE-EXISTING bug (present since E1.3) in flutter_pear_bare's core IPC transport -- BasicMessageChannel<ByteBuffer> + Flutter's BinaryCodec silently delivers EMPTY data to Dart on every native-to-Dart send on this Flutter/Android setup (matches the historical flutter/flutter#19849 engine bug class). Bisected conclusively: a hardcoded 5-byte buffer sent via BinaryCodec arrived as zero-length; the identical bytes sent via a fresh StandardMessageCodec channel arrived correctly. Every previous 'clean boot, no crash, Fully drawn' validation throughout this entire project (E1.3, E1.4, E2.1-E2.4, E4.1, E4.3) never actually proved a real byte round trip -- the M0 UI renders regardless of whether Pear.start() ever resolves, and nothing previously waited past the E2.2 RPC timeout to notice a hang. Fixed by switching flutter_pear_bare/ipc to StandardMessageCodec on both the Kotlin (FlutterPearBarePlugin.kt) and Dart (bare_worklet.dart) sides, sending/receiving plain ByteArray/Uint8List instead of raw ByteBuffer/ByteData. Retroactive correction notes added to flutter_pear-vyw.3, vyw.4, 7be.1-7be.4, 47k.1, 47k.3 acknowledging their on-device validation claims were unproven; their own Dart-level test/analyze validation remains valid and unaffected. Filed flutter_pear-5rl to track the real fix (device/emulator integration testing) so this class of bug can't recur silently.

REVIEW (independent agent, high-depth) caught a second real bug in my OWN first-draft fix: making PearRpc._nextId a  counter (my initial 'fix' for cross-generation id-collision safety) does NOT survive a Dart hot restart -- confirmed hot restart resets ALL static/isolate state while the NATIVE worklet keeps running with in-flight stale calls, and critically, on a same-worklet hot-restart-reattach (as opposed to a kill+restart) the envelope nonce is UNCHANGED too, so nonce-checking responses wouldn't have caught a collision either. Fixed by switching id generation to RANDOM (dart:math Random, checked for local non-collision against _pending) instead of any sequential scheme, static or not -- this closes the collision risk for BOTH the hot-restart-reattach and kill+restart scenarios uniformly, regardless of whether the nonce changed. Also fixed: bundleVersion's hash now includes package-lock.json (an npm dependency bump alone wouldn't have changed the version before); Pear.start() now wraps its whole worklet/rpc selection in try/catch so a failure fetching attach.info (e.g. timeout on a slow boot) disposes+terminates whatever was just constructed instead of leaking an orphaned worklet+PearRpc; added a one-line doc comment acknowledging the boot-time 'joining' lifecycle event is expected (harmless) to be dropped pre-session.

TESTS: schema_test.dart covers the 2 new constants + JS mirror cross-check. rpc_test.dart: _FakeWorklet now stamps a mutable sessionNonce on every frame; new tests for attach.info's response shape, a stale-generation event being dropped without side effects even post-session-establishment, and a response settling correctly regardless of nonce (protected by id uniqueness instead). 27/27 tests green (flutter_pear), 3/3 (flutter_pear_bare).

REVIEW: ran a 3-angle Workflow review (correctness verified against real vendored autobase source; fake-conformance comparing _FakeBase against the real index.js+recipes handlers; schema-conventions for doc/categorization consistency), each finding independently adversarially verified. 15/15 findings CONFIRMED, all fixed:
- CRITICAL: BASE_APPEND never validated op shape before base.append(), so a malformed/wrong-recipe op was silently accepted then silently dropped by apply()'s own MALFORMED_OP-skip logic, with zero error ever reaching the caller. Fixed: BASE_APPEND now calls recipe.validate() / validateAddWriter / validateRemoveWriter synchronously before base.append(), after normalizeAppend (so crdtMap's del auto-fill still runs first).
- CRITICAL: removeWriter's doc promised a scoped PearStorageException when Autobase refuses to remove the last indexer, but tracing the pinned autobase source showed that error is UNCAUGHT by default and crashes the entire worklet process (Autobase's _onError calls crashSoon() when no 'error' listener exists on the base, confirmed in node_modules/autobase/index.js). Fixed: BASE_OPEN now registers base.on('error', ...) so _onError closes just this one base instead -- every other open core/bee/drive/base in the worklet survives. Doc updated to describe the corrected, accurate behavior.
- Real race: crdtMap's normalizeAppend does a genuine async view read with no per-base call serialization, so an overlapping put-then-del on the same key could have the del observe a stale (pre-put) view. Fixed: BASE_APPEND now chains through a per-base appendQueue so calls on one base are strictly FIFO.
- Fake-conformance (multiple): the fake's lww/crdtMap tie-break used a hub-wide, call-arrival-order counter instead of each writer's own local sequence number, so a test's fixture data could see the fake pick the opposite winner from what the real isNewer/causal-order recipe would for the identical writer history -- fixed via per-(base,writer) local counters + writer-key tiebreak, matching the real crdtMap recipe's isNewer exactly. writerKey was per-worklet instead of per-base (real base.local.key is namespaced per base) -- fixed via a deterministic per-(worklet,base) derivation. addWriter/removeWriter bypassed the writer-admission check entirely, letting a non-writer self-admit (structurally impossible in real Autobase) -- fixed by gating the whole handler on writer membership first. removeWriter had no last-indexer protection and never read the indexer flag -- fixed by tracking indexers and refusing to remove the last one. baseReplicate fired a spurious watch notification even when a merge changed nothing (e.g. two empty bases) -- fixed by gating _notify() on an actual size change, matching bee/drive's own precedent. Name-derived key hash folded in the recipe name, diverging from the real key derivation (which ignores recipe) -- fixed to match. Documented (not re-architected, per the fake's own scope) that a base merge is a permanent fusion with no re-divergence path, unlike a real repeatedly-reconciling Autobase pair.
- Docs: corrected the class doc's malformedOp claim (only put/del/append/addWriter/removeWriter validate synchronously; get/range fail with a generic storage exception instead), added the missing "Prefer Pear.base" convention sentence to open()'s doc, and switched doc references to out-of-scope symbols (PearPairing, PearStorageException) from square brackets to backticks (dartdoc [Foo] only resolves within the importing library; base.dart/schema.dart don't import pairing.dart/exceptions.dart).

Native change (FlutterPearBarePlugin.kt): startWorklet() now returns whether it reattached to an already-running worklet or booted a fresh one -- this was previously invisible to Dart (the "start" method call always returned null regardless of which startWorklet() branch ran). The "start" MethodChannel response now carries {"reattached": bool}. BareWorklet gained a reattached getter surfacing this.

Pear.start() uses this signal to pick the RIGHT timeout for its first attach.info probe: PearRpcDefaults.attachHealthTimeout (a new, short 3s constant) only when reattached is true (a possibly-stuck already-running worklet genuinely deserves a quick, suspicious probe); the normal, longer PearRpcDefaults.callTimeout (10s) whenever it's a genuine fresh boot (first-ever launch, or the mandatory post-kill retry, which is ALWAYS a fresh boot since whatever was running just got terminated) -- real JS engine + native module init legitimately takes real time, and misclassifying that as "unhealthy" would make Pear.start() fail forever on a device merely slow to cold-boot. Also narrowed the "treat an attach.info failure as unhealthy" catch to specifically PearErrorCode.rpcTimeout -- any OTHER failure (e.g. the worklet self-reporting WORKLET_CRASHED) now propagates immediately instead of being silently retried as if merely slow, per CLAUDE.md's "errors must travel" non-negotiable.

REAL BUG FOUND AND FIXED while verifying the contract with tests: PearConnection.write() was keyed purely by the peer's public key (matching the real worklet's own peer-hex-keyed connectionChannels/connections maps) -- meaning a stale PearConnection reference held past a drop would silently start delivering AGAIN once the same peer reconnected, directly contradicting the "never silently starts working again" contract. Fixed with a local _closed flag on PearConnection itself (checked before ever making the RPC call, so this is enforced by the Dart object regardless of worklet state) and a new Dart-side-only PearErrorCode.connectionClosed (mirroring the existing rpcTimeout/workletDisposed pattern -- no schema.js mirror needed, consistent with every other Dart-synthesized code).

CRITICAL (caught by this ticket's own Workflow review, not by me): my first attempt at fix 1 also gated _connectTo's CONNECTED-state emission on a per-PEER "isNew" flag, but pear-end's real per-topic connectedPeers Set means a peer already connected via one shared topic must still get connected on a SECOND shared topic -- my first fix would have permanently stranded any topic after the first shared one. 3. disconnectFrom's connectionClose event fired unconditionally per topic while the sibling reconnecting state was correctly gated on the topic still being joined -- an asymmetry versus pear-end's real handler, which gates both together. Fixed by gating both on the same check.

v0.1 posture decided and documented in packages/flutter_pear/SECURITY_POSTURE.md:
- Storage location: identity/replication keys live in the worklet's own Corestore

  Dart never sees a private key, only public keys crossing the RPC boundary as PearKey.

  android:dataExtractionRules (API 31+) and android:fullBackupContent (legacy),
  each excluding pear-corestore/pear-bulk from cloud backup and device transfer.
  allowBackup=false (whole-app) was considered and rejected as too blunt for a
  library to impose on its host app.
- Reinstall/migration: NOTHING survives -- documented honestly. Android wipes
  internal storage on uninstall; a reinstalled app is an unrecognized new writer
  to every prior peer. No restore-identity path in v0.1.
- Invite TTL/replay: already resolved in E5.6 (ttl + inviteExpired + revoke-blocks-
  accept); referenced, not re-decided.
- Logging hygiene: zero-tolerance -- no print()/debugPrint() in flutter_pear's Dart
  source, no console.* in pear-end's JS source, enforced by a test, regardless of
  whether a given call site would touch key material.

Review: 3-angle Workflow review (correctness/test-rigor/conventions-simplicity,
19 sub-agents) found 16 CONFIRMED issues (deduped across angles), 0 refuted --
all genuine defects in the first draft, verified by the reviewers actually
running the tool against crafted fixtures, not just reading the code. Fixed:

- Marker matching was `startsWith('dart snippet')`, a bare prefix check that
  incorrectly swept up ```dart snippets```/```dart snippet-other```/etc. --
  now checks the info string's first two whitespace-separated tokens are
  exactly "dart"/"snippet".
- The fence parser silently DROPPED an unclosed snippet (0 issues, exit 0,
  broken example never analyzed) and silently MERGED two snippets when an
  earlier fence's own closing ``` was consumed by a later block's fence --
  both are now loud StateErrors: an unclosed fence at EOF throws, and any
  fence-looking line found inside an already-open fence throws (Dart syntax
  never contains a bare triple-backtick line, so this is a safe heuristic
  for "the fencing broke," not the snippet's own content).
- splitImports only recognized a *leading* contiguous run of import lines --
  a leading comment before the first import, or an import written mid-
  snippet, left the import statement inside the generated function body
  (invalid Dart, produces a wall of unrelated parse errors). Rewritten to
  scan the whole body and hoist every import line wherever it appears --
  simpler code than the original AND correct for both cases.
- main() only ever read args[0] with no way to check more than one README in
  one run; packages/flutter_pear/README.md would have silently gone
  unchecked forever. main() now loops over all provided paths, globally
  re-indexing snippets across files; CI now passes both READMEs.
- Snippet/SplitSnippet were hand-rolled classes bundling two values each
  (and had gained a real doc-comment asymmetry -- one class's fields
  documented, the other's not); replaced with record typedefs, which is both
  less code and structurally removes the spot that inconsistency lived in.
- Corrected a doc comment that gave the wrong reason for naming generated
  functions with a leading underscore (public_member_api_docs only applies
  under lib/, not tool/ -- verified against the lint rule's own source; the
  real reason is just keeping generated internals out of the way).

Reviewed and deliberately KEPT as designed (not defects): main() shelling out
to `dart analyze` itself rather than splitting codegen and analysis into two
separate CI steps -- this is literally the mechanism the ticket's own STEPS
describe ("runs dart analyze on them"), and splitting it wouldn't fix a bug,
just add a second CI step for marginal log-readability. Also explicitly NOT
handling CommonMark-style indented fences or a snippet body that legitimately
contains a literal ``` line (e.g. demonstrating Markdown) -- both are
documented `ponytail:` limitations in the tool's header: this repo's own
README doesn't nest snippets under list items, and a Dart P2P-networking
snippet is never going to need a literal triple-backtick sequence in its
body. Both now fail LOUD (a StateError) rather than silently, which is the
property that actually matters.

Root cause: flutter_pear_bare/android/build.gradle's linkNativeAddons Gradle Exec task ran bare-link against pear-end/node_modules AT A CONSUMER'S OWN BUILD TIME, located via a path relative to the CONSUMING APP's android/ directory -- only ever correct for flutter_pear_example (which happens to live inside this monorepo). Any real external consumer's onlyIf guard silently skipped linking with no error, shipping an APK missing every native addon; Pear.start() crashed instantly (AddonError: ADDON_NOT_FOUND, SIGABRT in bare-worklet).

FIX: moved bare-link to PACK time (dart run flutter_pear:pack's new linkNativeAddons in bin/pack.dart, called right after buildBundle). Commits the resulting per-ABI .so files (arm64-v8a, x86_64) as versioned artifacts under flutter_pear_bare/android/src/main/jniLibs/<abi>/ -- Android's standard jniLibs convention picks these up at any consumer's build time with zero custom Gradle task, matching the same 'committed, versioned artifact' treatment assets/pear-end.bundle already gets. Removed the old linkNativeAddons Exec task, its onlyIf guard, pearEndDir/nativeAddonsDir vars, and related wiring from build.gradle entirely; fetchBareKit (Bare Kit's own runtime binary fetch, unrelated/still correct) is untouched.
